title: Scratchpad Memory for Agents
concept: A working-memory buffer the agent writes intermediate reasoning to, separate from the main context window.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned about compacting tool outputs. What does 'compacting' a tool output mean?

options.text: Summarizing or trimming a verbose tool result before it re-enters the agent's context
Deleting the tool entirely so it can never be used again
Making the tool run twice as fast

exercises.explain: Compacting means shrinking a long, verbose tool result down to its essential parts — like a summary — so it doesn't overwhelm the agent's context window on the next step.

cards.title: Where Does an Agent 'Think Out Loud'?
cards.body: When an agent solves a tricky problem, it often needs space to jot down half-finished thoughts, calculations, or plans. But do those messy notes need to clutter the main conversation? Not if there's a separate notebook for them.

cards.title: What Is Scratchpad Memory?
cards.body: A scratchpad is a working-memory space where an agent writes down intermediate reasoning, notes, or partial calculations. It's kept separate from the main context window, which stays focused on the actual conversation and final results.

cards.title: Like Scratch Paper on a Math Test
cards.body: During a test, you don't write your messy scratch work on the final answer sheet — you use scratch paper to work things out, then only put the clean answer where it counts. A scratchpad works the same way for an agent.

cards.title: Multi-Step Math Problem
cards.body: An agent solving 'What's 15% tip on a $340 bill split 4 ways?' might jot in its scratchpad: 'Step 1: 15% of 340 = 51. Step 2: 340+51=391. Step 3: 391/4=97.75.' Only the final answer, $97.75 per person, needs to show up in the main context.

cards.title: Myth: Scratchpad = Main Context
cards.body: Myth: everything an agent writes must live in the main context window. Truth: a scratchpad is a separate buffer for rough reasoning, so the main context stays clean while the agent still gets room to think step-by-step.

cards.title: Main Context vs. Scratchpad
cards.body: Think of these as two separate notebooks: one is the polished conversation log, the other is where the agent scribbles its rough work before writing the clean version.

left.text: Holds the conversation history, key facts, and final answers the agent needs to keep track of

right.text: Holds messy intermediate reasoning, partial math, and draft plans that don't need to persist

exercises.prompt: Why would an agent use a scratchpad instead of putting all its intermediate reasoning directly in the main context window?

options.text: Because it keeps the main context focused and uncluttered while still giving the agent room to think through messy steps
Because the main context window can't hold any text at all
Because scratchpads make the agent respond slower on purpose
Because it's required by every AI model with no real benefit

exercises.explain: A scratchpad separates rough, in-progress reasoning from the polished context the agent actually needs for the conversation. This keeps the main context window smaller and more relevant, which helps the agent stay focused and avoid confusion.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A scratchpad is meant to be shown word-for-word to the end user as the final response.
exercises.explain: False — the scratchpad is for the agent's internal working notes. Only the polished, relevant conclusion typically gets shared with the user or passed into the main context.

exercises.sentence: The agent jotted its half-finished calculations in the scratchpad instead of putting them in the main context.
exercises.explain: The scratchpad is exactly this separate space — it holds the agent's rough intermediate reasoning so the main context window doesn't get cluttered with every partial thought.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three agent behaviors is most likely an AI-generated (rather than human-written) trace of scratchpad reasoning?

options.text: Step 1: Identify total cost. Step 2: Subtract discount. Step 3: Divide by number of people. Step 4: Round to two decimals.
ugh ok let me just guess 97 bucks lol, close enough
I think it's around $95 give or take, my gut says so

exercises.explain: The structured, methodical step-by-step breakdown is a telltale sign of AI-generated scratchpad reasoning — it's organized and systematic, unlike casual human guesses or gut feelings.

exercises.prompt: An agent is planning a multi-city trip. It needs to compare flight prices, hotel costs, and weather for 5 cities across several steps before giving a final recommendation. What's the best way to use context engineering here?

options.text: Write all comparisons and running totals into a scratchpad buffer, then only pull the final recommendation into the main context to show the user
Put every single price and weather report directly into the main context window as soon as it's fetched
Skip keeping track of anything and just guess the best city at the end
Ask the user to remember the numbers for the agent

exercises.explain: This is context engineering in action: compact tool outputs so they don't bloat the context, and use a scratchpad to hold messy intermediate work (comparisons, running totals) separately from the main context. Only the clean, final recommendation needs to enter the main conversation. This keeps the agent's 'visible' context small and focused while still letting it reason carefully behind the scenes. You've now completed this phase of your AIBites journey — congratulations on finishing LLM Application Engineering!
